topics.title: Microservices — Service Discovery, Circuit Breaker & Inter-Service Communication
topics.description: Microservices decompose a monolith into small, independently deployable services communicating over HTTP/gRPC/messaging. Key patterns: Service Discovery (Eureka/Consul) so services find each other without hardcoded URLs. Circuit Breaker (Resilience4j) stops cascading failures by short-circuiting repeated calls to a failing service. API Gateway (Spring Cloud Gateway) is the single entry point for routing, auth, rate limiting. Distributed tracing (Zipkin/Sleuth) tracks requests across services.
topics.story: A monolith is one restaurant: kitchen, cashier, and waiter in one room. Microservices are a food court: each stall (service) is independent. But now coordination is hard — how does the burger stall know where the drinks stall moved? (Service Discovery). What if the drinks stall is overwhelmed? (Circuit Breaker: stop ordering drinks for 30 seconds). Who checks IDs at the entrance? (API Gateway: one auth layer, not one per stall).
topics.analogy: Service Discovery = phone book that updates in real-time as services start/stop. Circuit Breaker = fuse box: too many failures → flip the fuse → fail fast without waiting → reset after 30 seconds (half-open). API Gateway = hotel receptionist: all guests (requests) go through reception, which routes to the right room (service). Feign Client = a typed HTTP client that looks like a local Java interface. Message queue (Kafka/RabbitMQ) = async walkie-talkie between services.
topics.memoryAnchor: DCCF architecture — Discovery (Eureka: services self-register, clients look up by name not IP). Communication (Feign: declarative HTTP client written as an interface; WebClient for reactive). Circuit Breaker states: CLOSED = normal traffic flows → OPEN = fail-fast when failure threshold breached (no calls attempted) → HALF-OPEN = probe with one request to check recovery. Fault tolerance. Saga pattern mnemonic: Each service owns its DB + Failure triggers compensating transaction (undo). Choreography = services react to events. Orchestration = one conductor tells services what to do. API Gateway = single front door for routing + auth + rate limiting. The 3 Microservice laws: own your data, be independently deployable, fail gracefully with fallbacks.
topics.firstPrinciples: Services must handle partial failure — any downstream call can fail, hang, or timeout. Circuit breaker pattern: count failures, if above threshold flip OPEN (fail immediately), after timeout move to HALF_OPEN (allow one probe request), if probe succeeds go CLOSED, else OPEN again. Service mesh (Istio) externalizes circuit breaker, retry, and mTLS from application code into the infrastructure layer.
topics.bruteForce: Hardcode service URLs in properties. No retry — user sees error. No circuit breaker — one slow service hangs threads, cascading timeout through the whole system. One big transaction spanning multiple services — distributed deadlock nightmare.
topics.optimizedApproach: Service registry for dynamic URL resolution. Circuit breaker + fallback for graceful degradation. Saga pattern for distributed transactions (choreography or orchestration). Event-driven async communication (Kafka) to decouple services. API Gateway for cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limit, logging). Correlation IDs + distributed tracing for debugging.
topics.whenToUse: Microservices: when teams are large enough to own services independently, when different services have different scaling needs, when you need independent deployment cycles. Monolith first for small teams (<10 devs). Circuit breaker: any synchronous inter-service call. Async messaging: when caller doesn't need immediate response. API Gateway: always — centralizes auth and routing.

examples.title: Feign Client + Circuit Breaker with Resilience4j
examples.explanation: Feign replaces manual RestTemplate calls — @FeignClient(name='user-service') resolves the URL via Eureka (no hardcoded host:port). @CircuitBreaker wraps the method: after 50% failures in the last 10 calls, the circuit flips OPEN and fallback is called immediately (no waiting). Retry runs before the circuit breaker counts — 3 retries on transient errors, but a real outage still flips the circuit after threshold. Fallback must have the same signature plus a Throwable/Exception parameter.
examples.realWorldUse: Netflix's Hystrix (now retired) pioneered this pattern. Resilience4j is the modern replacement. DevLearner could split into: topic-service, user-service, code-execution-service — each independently deployable. CircuitBreaker on code-execution: if the sandbox crashes, return 'execution unavailable' gracefully instead of hanging all threads.

examples.title: API Gateway with Spring Cloud Gateway
examples.explanation: Spring Cloud Gateway runs on WebFlux (reactive) — filters return Mono<Void> (non-blocking). GlobalFilter applies to all routes. The JWT filter validates token once at the gateway and passes user info downstream as headers (X-User-Id) — individual services trust this header without re-validating the JWT. lb://service-name uses Eureka for load-balanced routing. StripPrefix removes path prefix before forwarding.
examples.realWorldUse: Kong, AWS API Gateway, Nginx do similar gateway functions. DevLearner: adding a gateway would centralize JWT validation currently duplicated in each controller. Rate limiting at gateway: RateLimiter filter with Redis backend. Canary deployments: route 10% traffic to v2 via gateway weight routing.

problems.title: Circuit Breaker State Machine
problems.hint1: Track failureCount, state (enum), and openedAt (timestamp). In execute(), check state first.
problems.hint2: HALF_OPEN: allow one call. On success → CLOSED + reset counter. On failure → OPEN.
problems.hint3: OPEN check: if System.currentTimeMillis() - openedAt > 5000, transition to HALF_OPEN.
problems.testCases:
State after 3 failures: OPEN
Fast fail: Circuit is OPEN — failing fast
problems.sampleInput: Payment service down; 6/10 recent calls failed
problems.sampleOutput: Circuit OPEN; paymentFallback() returns queued response

problems.title: Service Discovery URL Resolution
problems.hint1: Map<String, List<String>> stores service name → list of URLs.
problems.hint2: Round-robin: keep a counter per service name. index = counter % urls.size().
problems.hint3: Use AtomicInteger per service for thread-safe round-robin counter.
problems.sampleOutput: HTTP GET inventory-service/api/inventory/product-123 — load balanced across instances

problems.title: Saga Pattern — Compensating Transactions
problems.hint1: Execute steps in order. If step N fails, run compensations for steps N-1 down to 1.
problems.hint2: Store completed step names in a list. On failure, iterate in reverse to compensate.
problems.hint3: Each step has a forward action and a compensation action (both Runnable).
problems.testCases:
Order created
Payment charged
Step 'ReserveInventory' failed: Out of stock
Running compensations...
Payment refunded
Order cancelled
problems.sampleInput: POST /api/orders creates order, publishes OrderEvent to Kafka
problems.sampleOutput: Order saved immediately; notification email sent asynchronously via Kafka consumer
